Now, there's three things that can happen here. They can say yes, which means you have a perfect lay-down sale. They can say no, which means they're just simply not interested, right? Now let's deal with these two first. These are very rare. People don't typically just say yes, right, at any game, right? It's very simple to lay down. We get them once in a while where someone's almost free sold before they even enter the encounter. They just yes, but she's no objections at all. But they're very rare. But even rarer still is it outright no. They say no, you give them a picture like no, I'm not interested. And the reason you don't get many no's is because you've already weaned those people out. Remember, you've just gone through a little bit. So, let's take that away. You've just gone through just... Ah, can you move back and forth? Okay. This is, can you read this shit, all right? You've just gone through this intensive intelligence gathering questions where you ask all these questions. They answered you thoroughly. If they didn't answer you, well, they didn't qualify. They didn't need your prop. You would have ended the sale. So, when I talk about the straight line transition here, it's not just talk. You actually, you know, read out the people who aren't right to your prop and who aren't interested in your prop. That was a proctagonal lesson I gave about a month ago, right? So, again, the key is that so because of that, the chance of you making the presentation to someone after you gather all these intelligence, now you actually showed them the solution to your prop to that problem, they all of a sudden say, nah, I don't want it. That does not happen. That almost never happens. So, they really very seldom say yes. They even more seldom say no. But what they say 97% of the time is maybe. And what maybe is, is a catch on for all the common objections. Let me think about it means maybe. Let me think about it. Let me call you that means maybe. Let me call you that. Then coming a maybe, I should have not the right time. I know. So, it's not a hard though. It's not a yes. It's a maybe. So, maybe is your catch on for all the common objections that we typically hear when we ask for the order for the first time. And this is when looping begins. So, we call it looping because what you actually do, now remember, you've given this airtight logical frame you created with your presentation. You ask for the order. They say, let me think about it. Right? And we know this. When someone says, let me think about it, it's a smoke screen for uncertainty. In other words, this is a very, very important concept here. Because it's going to really help you essentially deal with objections in the right emotional frame. The problem many sales perhaps, they go through all presentation. And the prospect says, let me think about it. Oh my God. I just did this whole thing again. Another one wants to think, oh, fuck. Jesus, this doesn't end. I just want to lay down. Why is everyone wanting to call speaking their life or when they're a captain towards the bed. And you start getting like, you lose your own state of certainty because you've gotten hit with an objection. So the key here, I can't stress this enough. The first takeaway here for a looping here, and please write this down, this is an important takeaway. You want to be expecting to be hearing a common objection. Expect, let me think about it. Expect, let me call you back. Expect a common objection. In fact, when you hear, I'm waiting for that common objection. Right? When I ask the owner, I'm waiting for it and they say, oh, it sounds good, George. I say, oh yeah, it sounds good. Let me think about it. And I'm like, ah, just as expected. Now I can roll up my sleeves and earn my freaking paycheck and show them what I can do because I'm the best at it. Notice, that is when I first get going. And the emotion you're transferring is circling. Certainly about yourself, certainly about your product, certainly about the coming behind the product, right? You're transferring that to the prospect. And the methodology, the system that you use is the straight line. But the certainty itself comes from you. The straight line doesn't generate certainty. The certainty has to come from you, my friend. The straight line transfers that certainty. It shows you what you're feeling inside and eventually transfer that to your prospect so they feel it too. And once they feel the same level of certainty you do, they buy. That's what happens. Now remember here. I'll use your, you can put a little bonus slide Q and A in about five minutes here, okay? But I want to, okay, so I'm going to button up. And then I'm going to add another element. There's your action threshold and pain threshold. These are your other two elements. Now, rather than go through, and I did looping the last one, I went through these and there was action threshold and pain threshold, right? Okay, in fact, I even did a whiteboard Wednesday. So, bone, we've eventually repost on this just on the member side here. I want to repost the whiteboard Wednesday I did about the interaction of pain and action threshold, right? So, you've got some of the links. Yeah, we'll send you the links next. I want you to review that after you watch this train. Round and get to those. I don't waste any of your time. We did this before, but it's an important point that we have these two other elements here. We have the action threshold and we have the pain threshold. But here's the tip here on Wednesday. When you start running your second and third loops and you start cutting to action threshold first, let me just tell you action threshold, right? Action threshold, what it means, it's the collective level of certainty that any individual, any prospect needs to be at before they feel comfortable enough to say yes. And we repeat, action threshold is the collective level of certainty. Meaning for all three tets, you have the product, you have you and you have the company. Your action threshold is how certain do you yourself act to be about the product, about the person selling you the product, about the company standing behind the product, all those wrapped up into a what, how certain do you need to feel before you can say yes? That's me, myself, I'm an easy target. I love the body shape. I have what's called the low action threshold. You can sell me pretty much anything. If I'm reasonably certain, I'm reasonably certain about those three things. My father, as I've said in the past, he's the opposite of me. My father has a very high action threshold, right? So before my father will buy, he has to be absolutely 10, 10, 10, and damn sure of it. I can get 8, 8, 7, and I still buy. Because my beliefs about buying are so, I was like, yeah, well, you know, I like to make my best decisions quickly and I trust most people and I just like to buy. I know I usually make good decisions when I buy and I buy quickly. Those are my beliefs. My dad's beliefs are very much the opposite. He did it. So action threshold, it's made up of the beliefs someone has about buying and it impacts how they make decisions, right? Now, the way we lower someone's action threshold, right, is by essentially using the power of language to interrupt their strategy they use for making buying things. Watch. People who buy at the end of the year, because I don't want to prolong too much, but it's at a very important point before we start Q&A, okay? Watch. So when you make a decision, you, me, anyone, bow and arch, anyone, right? Before that moment before you say yes or no, your brain runs these parallel loops. It goes, bro, what's the worst second possibly happening? If I say yes and buy this thing, let's say it turns out to be a total fraud piece of shit, scam, what's the, what's my downside? How much worse off will I be? And then at the same, on simultaneous, you know, your brain's terrible, it runs the positive movie. If I buy this and it works, how is my life going to look in the future? Well, I'd be at pain. How will I feel? You imagine yourself in the future feeling good. You run the upside movie, you run the downside movie. Now, here's what happens. Both me having the low action threshold and my father having the high action threshold, we both run those movies. The difference is I run a really, really long positive movie and a very short negative movie. My dad, on the other hand, runs a very long negative movie, very short positive movie. We run different movies. We run both, but I run off of a glass half full sort of guy. He's a glass half empty sort of guy. So he'll run this long toxic negative, oh, it was terrible, take a laugh at him, he gets a decision, and if he's right, oh yeah, maybe it'll be okay. I'm the opposite. You get, that's the strategy. Now, the way we interrupt that strategy is by running a language pattern that essentially hijacks it. I say to them, what's the worst that could possibly happen? Let's say I'm wrong, and I actually tell them the truth. What could happen if they buy and it doesn't work out? And it's going to be one thousandth as bad as they made it. In their mind, they exaggerate the negative downside. So I hijacked their strategy. Now, I might, and then I'm the opposite, I'll tell them all the one of the things that will happen, right? So that's what I'm doing. Now, this is not the purpose of what I'm saying. I want to go back a lot, a different angle here, because I talk about that in the White Board of Ways. Here's what I'm driving at. After I run, I call this an action, threshold lowering strategy. I hijack someone's movies. I tell them, listen, honestly, if I'm wrong, here's what's going to happen. And if I'm right, here's what's wrong. And then get this long positive upside movie. That's in the White Board of Ways Day. But here's what's not in White Board of Ways Day. After I'm done with that movie, I can't ask for the order again. You know why? Because certainty waits. I have to go back one more time and button up the three tens. And then ask for the order. Now I'll say it quickly and you'll watch this in the whiteboard Wednesday is that you must know the negotiating landscape. This is the biggest mistake people make is that before they enter a negotiation, they don't even know what a winning negotiation looks like. They don't know. They don't know like, okay, if they go to buy a car and they walk in there and they're like, no, I'm going to be a negotiate expert. And they lobel all the salesman to where the salesman is losing money on the economy. The guy couldn't sell to the people he wanted to. It's below his own price, right? It's below his own floor. So what do you do that? You waste your time, you waste your time, you're looking asshole, right? So you are asking for things and you think you won. They're like, great, done. And then like that, more on, he didn't know. He didn't know twice as much. That's why I need my negotiating landscape. Make sure you know what a good deal looks like. This is why mentorship is so important. So do some research online, ask somebody who knows, okay, what stands the busier and find out like, you know, what should I be asking for? What is a win? What does it look like? What will I never get? And what can I get? Okay, that's number one. Is understand the power of infangibles. This is one of the things I do very well when I negotiate. So I always identify beforehand, what are the crucial things that matter to someone else? They're important to someone else. But they don't have really monetary value to me. Things like, I'll give you an example, stratum back in the day. You know what the most important thing was? A fucking parking spot. We had talkers. I was over here, my father was over there, Danny and Kenny, right? How far you went from me, you know, was your hierarchy. If you gave someone a promote guide, moving your phone, you're like fucking worth a million dollars. That's an example of an intangible. Certain title, I have people with a, or I'd like to be global sales manager. And they'll be like, I don't care. I'll take this to draw. If you give me global sales manager, you're like, you use it on my phone. All right, yeah. Now me, I've never cared about title. I need a title. Right. But some people are so title-centric, they keep the exact same washing. They put it on a certain benefit program. It doesn't really cost you that much, but means a lot to someone else. Those intangibles are a deal. They differ from deal to deal. Identify all the intangibles. Wherever. This is called strategic preparation. Before the negotiation starts, you want to prepare, find out the landscape, find out what the intangibles are. And then you save those intangibles to the end. And when you're close, but can't get the person to the line, you give them things that matter a lot to them, but don't cost you anything. That's the key. This might sound a bit like, oh, come on, you're speaking nice, but it's actually true, right? In a truly great negotiation, a truly winning negotiation, both sides feel like they won. I don't mean that in the typical sort of, you know, gay negotiation. Everyone should be happy. No, here's the problem. That because it was a perfect example of someone that I was in a negotiation, they didn't know the landscape. They gave me more than I should have gotten. Steve Mann shoes is the one I was at. He gave me, he gave me $4 million, and I took 85% of his company and left him at 15. He was happy. He should have never given away more than 50%. He didn't know better. And I made a mistake. It was my fault to take advantage of him. It wasn't illegal. It wasn't even unethical, but it was unsustainable. Because once I get to a few years, he's like, what the fuck, this is crazy. He owns the whole thing, I'm doing all the work. And then those things always ensue you and fuck you and have the sessions all split. You want to, you get that? So it's not always what's ethical or what's legal, it's what's sustainable. It's a great negotiation, and it truly wouldn't be negotiation. The result is sustainable for both sides. They won't look back at it and think it's a disaster. Another great example, the tree of Versailles. They created an outcome where it was when we won the war, we didn't destroy you, we let you rise again, and now we're all friends.
